content: The variation of density of a solid cylindrical rod of cross sectional area $\alpha$ and length $L$ is $\rho=\rho_0 \frac{x^2}{L^2}$, where $x$ is the distance from one end of the rod. The position of its centre of mass from one end $(x=0)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{array}{ll} \because & \frac{d m}{d v}=\rho \\ \Rightarrow & d m=\alpha d x \rho\,\,\,\, [\because d v=a d t]\\ \Rightarrow & d m=\frac{\alpha \rho_0 x^2}{L^2} d x \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Now, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & X_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{\int x d m}{\int d m}=\frac{\frac{\alpha \rho_0}{L^2} \int_0^L x^3 d x}{\frac{\alpha \rho_0}{L^2} \int_0^L x^2 d x} \\ & X_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{\left[\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^L}{\left[\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^L}=\frac{3}{4} L \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: A ball falls from a height $h$ upon a fixed horizontal floor. The co-efficient of restitution for the collision between the ball and the floor is ' $e$ '. The total distance covered by the ball before coming to rest is [neglect the air resistance]
explanation:
When you drop the ball from height $h$, it hits the floor and rebounds to a height

$ h_1 \;=\; e^2\,h. $

Each subsequent bounce reaches $e^2$ times the previous height, so the up-and-down distances form a geometric series.

Total distance $D$ is

$ D \;=\; h \;+\; 2\bigl(h_1 + h_2 + h_3 + \cdots\bigr) \;=\; h \;+\; 2h\bigl(e^2 + e^4 + e^6 + \cdots\bigr). $

The infinite sum $e^2 + e^4 + e^6 + \cdots$ equals $\dfrac{e^2}{1 - e^2}$. Hence

$ D \;=\; h \;+\; 2h\;\frac{e^2}{1 - e^2} \;=\; h\;\frac{1 - e^2 + 2e^2}{1 - e^2} \;=\; h\;\frac{1 + e^2}{1 - e^2}. $

That matches option B:

$ \boxed{\frac{1 + e^2}{1 - e^2}\;h}. $

content: The position of the centre of mass of the uniform plate as shown in the figure is

A particle of mass m is projected at a velocity u, making an angle $$\theta$$ with the horizontal (x-axis). If the angle of projection $$\theta$$ is varied keeping all other parameters same, then magnitude of angular momentum (L) at its maximum height about the point of projection varies with $$\theta$$ as,
explanation:
$$r_{\perp}=H_{\max }=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}, v=u \cos \theta$$

Angular momentum at the maximum height about point $$O$$ is

$$\begin{aligned} L & =m v r_{\perp}=m(u \cos \theta)\left(\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}\right) \\ \therefore \quad L & =\left(\frac{m u^3}{2 g}\right) \cdot \sin ^2 \theta \cos \theta \end{aligned}$$

As, $$\sin ^2 \theta$$ varies from 0 to 1 between 0 to $$\pi / 2$$.

$$\cos \theta$$ varies from 1 to 0 between 0 to $$\pi / 2$$.

$$\therefore \sin ^2 \theta \cdot \cos \theta$$ varies from 0 to 0 between 0 to $$\pi / 2$$.

At $$\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}, \sin ^2 \theta \cdot \cos \theta=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{4}$$

$$\therefore$$ Only option (d) is correct.

content: There are n elastic balls placed on a smooth horizontal plane. The masses of the balls are $$\mathrm{m}, \frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}, \frac{\mathrm{m}}{2^{2}}, \ldots \frac{\mathrm{m}}{2^{\mathrm{n}-1}}$$ respectively. If the first ball hits the second ball with velocity $$\mathrm{v}_{0}$$, then the velocity of the $$\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$$ ball will be,
explanation:
Let the collision of two balls

$$\therefore$$ By conservation of momentum,

$$m v_0=m v_1+\frac{m}{2} v_2$$

$$\therefore \quad v_0=v_1+\frac{v_2}{2}$$ ..... (i)

For elastic collision, $$e=1=\frac{v_2-v_1}{v_0-0}$$

$$\therefore \quad v_0=v_2-v_1$$ .... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3 v_2}{2} & =2 v_0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & v_2=\frac{4 v_0}{3} \end{aligned}$$

Similarly, the velocity of $$3 \mathrm{rd}$$ ball, $$v_3=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 v_0$$

$$\therefore$$ Velocity of $$n$$th ball, $$v_n=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{n-1} v_0$$

content: A particle is moving in an elliptical orbit as shown in figure. If $$\overrightarrow p $$, $$\overrightarrow L $$ and $$\overrightarrow r $$ denote the linear momentum, angular momentum and position vector of the particle (from focus O) respectively at a point A, then the direction of $$\overrightarrow \alpha $$ = $$\overrightarrow p $$ $$\times$$ $$\overrightarrow L $$ is along.
explanation:
At position A, direction of $$\overrightarrow p $$ is along + ve Y-axis.

At position A, direction of $$\overrightarrow L $$ is along + ve Z-axis because $$\overrightarrow L $$ = $$\overrightarrow r $$ $$\times$$ $$\overrightarrow p $$ and $$\overrightarrow r $$ is along + ve X-axsis.

Thus, $$\overrightarrow \alpha $$ = $$\overrightarrow p $$ $$\times$$ $$\overrightarrow L $$

$$ = \widehat i \times \widehat k$$

$$ = - \widehat j$$ or $$-$$ve Y-axis.

content: A body of mass m is thrown with velocity u from the origin of a co-ordinate axes at an angle $$\theta$$ with the horizon. The magnitude of the angular momentum of the particle about the origin at time t when it is at the maximum height of the trajectory is proportional to
explanation:
Maximum height of projectile,

$$h = {{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {2g}}$$ ...... (i)

At maximum height velocity of projectile,

$$v = u\cos \theta $$

$$\therefore$$ Momentum at highest point $$ = mu\cos \theta $$

$$\therefore$$ Angular momentum about origin,

$$p = mu\cos \theta \times h$$

$$ = mu\cos \theta \times {{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {2g}}$$

$$ = {{m{u^3}\cos \theta {{\sin }^2}\theta } \over {2g}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow p \propto {u^3}$$

content: Three particles, each of mass 'm' grams situated at the vertices of an equilateral $$\Delta$$ABC of side 'a' cm (as shown in the figure). The moment of inertia of the system about a line AX perpendicular to AB and in the plane of ABC in g-cm2 units will be
explanation:
Moment of inertia about line $$AX = m{a^2} + m{d^2}$$

We know that,

$$\sin \theta = {d \over a}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = {d \over a}$$ ($$\because$$ $$\theta = 30^\circ $$)

$$ \Rightarrow d = {a \over 2}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${I_{Ax}} = m{a^2} + m{\left( {{a \over 2}} \right)^2}$$

$$ = m{a^2} + {{m{a^2}} \over 4} = {5 \over 4}m{a^2}$$

content: A uniform thin rod of length L, mass m is lying on a smooth horizontal table. A horizontal impulse P is suddenly applied perpendicular to the rod at one end. The total energy of the rod after the impulse is
explanation:
The given impulse acts as both linear and an angular impulse.

Linear impulse = P = mvCM (where, vCM = velocity of centre at mass of rod)

Angular impulse = $$P \times {L \over 2} = {I_{CM}}\omega $$

where, $${I_{CM}} = {{m{L^2}} \over {12}}$$

and $$\omega$$ is angular velocity at rod about centre of mass,

i.e. $$\omega = {{PL} \over {2{I_{CM}}}}$$

Kinetic energy

$$ = \left[ {{1 \over 2}mv_{CM}^2 + {1 \over 2}{I_{CM}}{\omega ^2}} \right]$$

$$ = \left[ {{1 \over 2} \times m \times {{\left( {{P \over m}} \right)}^2} + {1 \over 2} \times {{m{L^2}} \over {12}} \times {{{P^2}{L^2} \times 144} \over {4 \times {m^2} \times {L^2}}}} \right]$$

$$ = {{{P^2}} \over {2m}}[1 + 3]$$

$$ = {{2{P^2}} \over m}$$

content: Centre of mass (CM) of three particles of masses 1 kg, 2 kg and 3 kg lies at the point (1, 2, 3) and CM of another system of particles of 3 kg and 2 kg lies at the point ($$-$$1, 3, $$-$$2). Where should we put a particle of mass 5 kg, so that the CM of entire system lies at the CM of the first system?
explanation:
Centre of mass of three particles (i.e. 1 kg, 2kg and 3kg)

$${{1{r_1} + 2{r_2} + 3{r_3}} \over 6} = [1\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k]$$

$$\because$$ $$\left[ {CM = {{{m_1}{r_1} + {m_2}{r_2} + {m_3}{r_3}} \over {{m_1} + {m_2} + {m_3}}}} \right]$$

where, r1, r2 and r3 are position vector of mass 1 kg, 2kg and 3kg, respectively.

Now, centre of mass of two particles (i.e. 3kg and 2 kg)

$${{3{r_4} + 2{r_5}} \over 5} = [ - \widehat i + 3\widehat j - 2\widehat k]$$

where, r4 and r5n are position vector of mass 1 kg and 2 kg.

Now, according to question, if 5 kg mass is added to whole particle system, then entire mass lies on CM of first system,

$${{1{r_1} + 2{r_2} + 3{r_3} + 3{r_4} + 2{r_5} + 5{r_6}} \over {16}} = [1\widehat i + 2\widehat j + 3\widehat k]$$

where, r6 is position vector of 5 kg mass.

$$ \Rightarrow 6\widehat i + 12\widehat j + 18\widehat k - 5\widehat i + 15\widehat j - 10\widehat k + 5{r_6} = 16\widehat i + 32\widehat j + 48\widehat k$$

$$ \Rightarrow 5{r_6} = 15\widehat i + 5\widehat j + 40\widehat k$$

$$ \Rightarrow {r_6} = 3\widehat i + \widehat j + 8\widehat k$$

Hence, position of 5 kg mass is (3, 1, 8).

content: A solid spherical ball and a hollow spherical ball of two different materials of densities $$\rho $$<sub>1</sub> and $$\rho $$<sub>2</sub> respectively have same outer radii and same mass. What will be the ratio, the moment of inertia (about an axis passing through the centre) of the hollow sphere to that of the solid sphere?
explanation:
$$ \therefore $$ $${M_1} = {\rho _1}{4 \over 3}\pi {R^3}$$ and $${M_2} = {\rho _2}{4 \over 3}\pi ({R^3} - {r^3})$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${\rho _1}{4 \over 3}\pi {R^3} = {\rho _2}{4 \over 3}\pi ({R^3} - {r^3})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\rho _1}{R^3} = {\rho _2}({R^3} - {r^3})$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{{\rho _1}} \over {{\rho _2}}} = 1 - {{{r^3}} \over {{R^3}}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${r \over R} = {\left( {1 - {{{\rho _1}} \over {{\rho _2}}}} \right)^{{1 \over 3}}}$$ ...(i)

Moment of inertia,

$${{{I_H}} \over {{I_S}}} = {{{2 \over 5}{M^2}\left[ {{{{R^5} - {r^5}} \over {{R^3} - {r^3}}}} \right]} \over {{2 \over 5}{M_1}{R^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{\rho _2}{4 \over 3}\pi ({R^3} - {r^3})\left[ {{{{R^5} - {r^5}} \over {({R^3} - {r^3})}}} \right]} \over {{\rho _1}{4 \over 3}\pi {R^3}{R^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{\rho _2}} \over {{\rho _1}}}\left[ {{{{R^5} - {r^5}} \over {{R^2}}}} \right] = {{{\rho _2}} \over {{\rho _1}}}\left[ {1 - {{\left( {{r \over R}} \right)}^5}} \right]$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${{{I_H}} \over {{I_S}}} = {{{\rho _2}} \over {{\rho _1}}}\left[ {1 - {{\left( {1 - {{{\rho _1}} \over {{\rho _2}}}} \right)}^{{5 \over 3}}}} \right]$$

content: Two particles A and B (both initially at rest) start moving towards each other under a mutual force of attraction. At the instant, when the speed of A is v and the speed of B is 2v, the speed of the centre of mass is
explanation:
In the absence of external force the speed of centre of mass is always zero i.e.

ucm = 0

$$ \because $$ Fext = 0 then,

vcm = 0 always

content: Two bodies of masses m<sub>1</sub> and m<sub>2</sub> are separated by a distance R. The distance of the centre of mass of the bodies from the mass m<sub>1</sub> is
explanation:
The bodies are separated by a distance R, so the coordinates of m1 and m2 will be (0, 0) and (R, 0)



From the formula of centre of mass,

$${X_{cm}} = {{{m_1}{x_1} + {m_2}{x_2}} \over {{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

$$ = {{{m_1} \times 0 + {m_2} \times R} \over {{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

$${X_{cm}} = {{{m_2}.R} \over {{m_1} + {m_2}}}$$

content: A uniform rod $$\mathrm{AB}$$ of length $$1 \mathrm{~m}$$ and mass $$4 \mathrm{~kg}$$ is sliding along two mutually perpendicular frictionless walls OX and OY. The velocity of the two ends of the $$\operatorname{rod} \mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}$$ are $$3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$ and $$4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$ respectively, as shown in the figure. Then which of the following statement(s) is/are correct?

options.content: The velocity of the centre of mass of the rod is $$2.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$$.
Rotational kinetic energy of the rod is $$\frac{25}{6}$$ joule.
The angular velocity of the rod is $$5 \mathrm{~rad} / \mathrm{s}$$ clockwise.
The angular velocity of the rod is $$5 \mathrm{~rad} / \mathrm{s}$$ anticlockwise.

explanation:
$\begin{aligned} & \omega=\frac{v_A}{C A}=\frac{v_B}{C B} \\\\ & \omega^2=\frac{v_A^2}{C A^2}=\frac{v_B^2}{C B^2}=\frac{v_A^2+v_B^2}{L^2}=\frac{3^2+4^2}{1^2} \\\\ & \omega=5 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s} \Rightarrow(D)\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & C G=\frac{1}{2}, \quad \omega=\frac{v_G}{C G}, \quad v_G=2.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \Rightarrow(A) \\\\ & \frac{1}{2} I_c \omega^2=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{12} \mathrm{~mL}^2+m\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2\right] \omega^2\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{3} m L^2\right) \omega^2 \\\\ & =\frac{1}{6} \mathrm{~mL}^2 \omega^2 \\\\ & =\frac{1}{6} \times 4 \times 1^2 \times 25=\frac{50}{3} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} m v_G^2=\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \frac{25}{4}=\frac{25}{2} \mathrm{~J} \\\\ & \frac{50}{3}-\frac{25}{2}=\frac{100-75}{6}=\frac{25}{6} \mathrm{~J} \Rightarrow(B)\end{aligned}$

content: A small steel ball bounces on a steel plate held horizontally. On each bounce the speed of the ball arriving at the plate is reduced by a factor e (coefficient of restitution) in the rebound, so that V<sub>upward</sub> = eV<sub>downward</sub>. If the ball is initially dropped from a height of 0.4 m above the plate and if 10 seconds later the bouncing ceases, the value of e is
explanation:
Given, steel ball bounces on a steel plate held horizontally, so

$${V_{upward}} = e{V_{downward}}$$

According to the relation,

$${h_n} = {e^{2n}}h$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$t = \sqrt {{{2h} \over g}} + 2\sqrt {{{2h{e^2}} \over g}} + 2\sqrt {{{2h{e^4}} \over g}} + ....$$

$$ = \sqrt {{{2h} \over g}} [1 + 2e + 2{e^2} + ...]$$

$$ = \sqrt {{{2h} \over g}} \left[ {{{1 + e} \over {1 - e}}} \right]$$

$$ \therefore $$ t = 10 sec

$$ \therefore $$ $$10 = \sqrt {{{2(0.4)} \over {10}}} \left( {{{1 + e} \over {1 - e}}} \right)$$

Solving above relation

$$ \therefore $$ $$e = {{25\sqrt 2 - 1} \over {25\sqrt 2 + 1}} \approx {{17} \over {18}}$$
